papers.title: Gaia DR2 in 6D: Searching for the fastest stars in the Galaxy
papers.author_names: T. Marchetti E. M. Rossi A. G. A. Brown

abstract.text: We search for the fastest stars in the subset of stars with radial velocity measurements of the second data release (DR2) of the European Space Agency mission Gaia. Starting from the observed positions, parallaxes, proper motions, and radial velocities, we construct the distance and total velocity distribution of more than $7$ million stars in our Milky Way, deriving the full 6D phase space information in Galactocentric coordinates. These information are shared in a catalogue, publicly available at http://home.strw.leidenuniv.nl/~marchetti/research.html. To search for unbound stars, we then focus on stars with a probability greater than $50 \\\\%$ of being unbound from the Milky Way. This cut results in a clean sample of $125$ sources with reliable astrometric parameters and radial velocities. Of these, $20$ stars have probabilities greater than 80 $\\\\%$ of being unbound from the Galaxy. On this latter sub-sample, we perform orbit integration to characterize the stars' orbital parameter distributions. As expected given the relatively small sample size of bright stars, we find no hypervelocity star candidates, stars that are moving on orbits consistent with coming from the Galactic Centre. Instead, we find $7$ hyper-runaway star candidates, coming from the Galactic disk. Surprisingly, the remaining $13$ unbound stars cannot be traced back to the Galaxy, including two of the fastest stars (around $700$ km/s). If conformed, these may constitute the tip of the iceberg of a large extragalactic population or the extreme velocity tail of stellar streams.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] Using Gaia DR2 data, the authors construct a 6D phase space catalog of over 7 million stars to identify unbound stellar candidates. They find no hypervelocity stars (HVSs) consistent with ejection from the Galactic Center, but identify 7 hyper-runaway stars originating from the Galactic disk and 13 unbound stars with velocities exceeding 700 km s⁻¹—some of which cannot be traced back to the Milky Way, suggesting possible extragalactic origins or extreme stellar streams.

source_fields.title: Gaia DR2 in 6D: Searching for the fastest stars in the Galaxy

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This study identifies 20 high-velocity stars with >80% probability of being unbound from the Milky Way, none of which originate from the inner galaxy, with 7 being hyper-runaway star candidates and 13 potentially originating from the Large Magellanic Cloud or tidally disrupted dwarf galaxies, while uncertainties in parallax and possible bound status remain open questions. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify and characterize hypervelocity stars by combining high-precision kinematic data with dynamical modeling of Galactic center interactions, addressing the long-standing problem of accurately detecting and understanding the formation mechanisms of stars with velocities exceeding 1000 km s⁻¹ that escape the Milky Way. High Velocity Stars in Gaia DR2: This section describes the identification and selection of 125 high-velocity stars from Gaia DR2 using stringent astrometric and radial velocity quality cuts to minimize contamination by instrumental artifacts, resulting in a clean sample of kinematic outliers with high unbound probability. Appendix D Global Parallax Offset: Including the $-0.029$ mas global parallax offset lowers inferred distances and velocities, resulting in 14 of the 20 high-velocity stars now having $P_{\\mathrm{ub}}>50\\%$ and 4 having $P_{\\mathrm{ub}}>80\\%$. Appendix E Systematic Errors in Parallax: This section discusses the impact of incorporating systematic parallax errors into Gaia DR2 parallax uncertainties, using a calibrated correction model to update distances, velocities, and unbound probabilities for high-velocity star candidates, with a conservative estimate that underestimates true distances. Distance and Total Velocity Determination: This section describes the determination of stellar distances and total velocities from Gaia DR2 data, focusing on stars with low parallax error ($0<f\\leq0.1$) by using parallax inversion and Monte Carlo sampling to model astrometric parameters, then correcting for solar and local standard of rest motions to derive Galactic rest frame velocities. The Total Velocity Distribution of Stars in Gaia DR2: This section presents the total velocity distribution of stars in the Galactic rest frame from Gaia DR2, focusing on a high-precision sample with velocity errors below 30%, and identifies a persistent high-velocity tail that may include unbound or hyper-velocity stars. Appendix A Choice of the Prior Probability on Distances: This paper addresses the challenge of accurately estimating distances for bright stars with large parallax errors in Gaia DR2 by proposing and validating an exponentially decreasing prior with a scale length of 2600 pc, positioning itself within the broader context of distance inference and systematic error mitigation in large-scale astronomical surveys. Appendix B Content of the Distance and Velocity Catalogue: The distance and velocity catalogue provides median distance and velocity estimates with 16th and 84th percentile uncertainties, derived from distributions, with entries labeled 1 representing new measurements and entries labeled 2 sourced from Gaia DR2. Unbound Stars: Hypervelocity and Hyper Runaway Star Candidates: This section analyzes 20 high-velocity star candidates with high unbound probabilities, finding no hypervelocity stars (HVS) as their minimum crossing radii exceed 1 kpc, and identifies 13 potential extragalactic candidates with low Milky Way origin probabilities ($P_{\\mathrm{MW}} < 0.5$). Appendix C List of High Velocity Stars with $0.5<P_{\\mathrm{ub}}\\leq 0.8$.: This section presents a catalog of 105 high-velocity stars with $0.5 < P_{\\mathrm{ub}} \\leq 0.8$, including their Gaia identifiers, distances, total Galactic rest-frame velocities, and boost probabilities, sorted by decreasing $P_{\\mathrm{ub}}$.

abstract: We search for the fastest stars in the subset of stars with radial velocity measurements of the second data release (DR2) of the European Space Agency mission Gaia.
To search for unbound stars, we then focus on stars with a probability greater than $50 \\\\%$ of being unbound from the Milky Way.
Of these, $20$ stars have probabilities greater than 80 $\\\\%$ of being unbound from the Galaxy.
As expected given the relatively small sample size of bright stars, we find no hypervelocity star candidates, stars that are moving on orbits consistent with coming from the Galactic Centre.
Surprisingly, the remaining $13$ unbound stars cannot be traced back to the Galaxy, including two of the fastest stars (around $700$ km/s).
title: Gaia DR2 in 6D: Searching for the fastest stars in the Galaxy

catalog_assessment.evidence: Constructs and shares a public catalog of 7 million stars with full 6D phase space info from Gaia DR2, identifies 125 unbound star candidates, and provides a clean sample of 125 sources with reliable parameters.

papers.title: Revisiting hypervelocity stars after Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Douglas Boubert James Guillochon Keith Hawkins Idan Ginsburg N. Wyn Evans Jay Strader

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars are intriguing rare objects traveling at speeds large enough to be unbound from the Milky Way. Several mechanisms have been proposed for producing them, including the interaction of the Galaxy's super-massive black hole (SMBH) with a binary; rapid mass-loss from a companion to a star in a short-period binary; the tidal disruption of an infalling galaxy and finally ejection from the Large Magellanic Cloud. While previously discovered high-velocity early-type stars are thought to be the result of an interaction with the SMBH, the origin of high-velocity late type stars is ambiguous. The second data release of Gaia (DR2) enables a unique opportunity to resolve this ambiguity and determine whether any late-type candidates are truly unbound from the Milky Way. In this paper, we utilize the new proper motion and velocity information available from DR2 to re-evaluate a collection of historical data compiled on the newly-created Open Fast Stars Catalog. We find that almost all previously-known high-velocity late-type stars are most likely bound to the Milky Way. Only one late-type object (LAMOST J115209.12+120258.0) is unbound from the Galaxy. Performing integrations of orbital histories, we find that this object cannot have been ejected from the Galactic centre and thus may be either debris from the disruption of a satellite galaxy or a disc runaway.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Gaia DR2 proper motions reveal that nearly all previously identified late-type hypervelocity stars are bound to the Milky Way; only one candidate (LAMOST J115209.12+120258.0) is unbound, and orbital integrations rule out ejection from the Galactic center, suggesting it may originate from satellite galaxy disruption or disc runaway dynamics.

source_fields.title: Revisiting hypervelocity stars after Gaia DR2

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: After Gaia DR2, the vast majority of hypervelocity star candidates—especially late-type FGKM stars—are now confidently bound to the Milky Way (with 464 out of 514 having $P_{\\mathrm{bound}} > 0.5$), while early-type OBA stars show a significant increase in unbound probabilities, and only 38 candidates remain unbound ($P_{\\mathrm{bound}} < 0.5$), primarily among B/A dwarfs and rare stellar types. Conclusions: Gaia's precise astrometry reveals that most historical late-type high-velocity stars are bound to the Milky Way, highlighting its superiority over ground-based data and paving the way for future discoveries of hypervelocity stars. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel, Gaia-based method to robustly identify late-type hypervelocity stars by leveraging precise proper motions and parallaxes to distinguish true hypervelocity candidates from those misclassified due to large proper motions alone, thereby addressing the problem of unreliable hypervelocity star claims in the literature. Appendix A The Open Fast Stars Catalog: The Open Fast Stars Catalog curates and systematically combines spectroscopic and astrometric data from the literature with Gaia measurements to enable rigorous analysis of hypervelocity star candidates, including automated calculations of Galactic rest-frame velocities, escape speeds, and derived quantities to assess boundedness and observability. History of searches for late-type hypervelocity stars: Prior to Gaia DR2, numerous late-type hypervelocity star candidates were identified using ground-based proper motions and spectroscopic data, but many were later found to be erroneous due to inaccurate proper motions or bound orbits, highlighting the need for precise parallaxes and proper motions to reliably confirm such stars.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars are intriguing rare objects traveling at speeds large enough to be unbound from the Milky Way.
While previously discovered high-velocity early-type stars are thought to be the result of an interaction with the SMBH, the origin of high-velocity late type stars is ambiguous.
In this paper, we utilize the new proper motion and velocity information available from DR2 to re-evaluate a collection of historical data compiled on the newly-created Open Fast Stars Catalog.
We find that almost all previously-known high-velocity late-type stars are most likely bound to the Milky Way. Only one late-type object (LAMOST J115209.12+120258.0) is unbound from the Galaxy.
title: Revisiting hypervelocity stars after Gaia DR2

catalog_assessment.evidence: Re-evaluates historical data compiled in the Open Fast Stars Catalog using Gaia DR2 proper motions, analyzes previously-known high-velocity late-type stars, and identifies one unbound candidate with specific ID.

papers.title: A hypervelocity star with a Magellanic origin
papers.author_names: Denis Erkal Douglas Boubert Alessia Gualandris N. Wyn Evans Fabio Antonini

abstract.text: Using proper motion measurements from Gaia DR2, we probe the origin of 26 previously known hypervelocity stars (HVSs) around the Milky Way. We find that a significant fraction of these stars have a high probability of originating close to the Milky Way centre, but there is one obvious outlier. HVS3 is highly likely to be coming almost from the centre of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). During its closest approach, $21.1^{+6.1}_{-4.6}$ Myr ago, it had a relative velocity of $870^{+69}_{-66}$ kms$^{-1}$ with respect to the LMC. This large kick velocity is only consistent with the Hills mechanism, requiring a massive black hole at the centre of the LMC. This provides strong direct evidence that the LMC itself harbours a massive black hole of at least $4\\\\times 10^3 -10^4 M_\\\\odot$.

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] Using Gaia DR2 proper motion data, the authors reanalyze the origins of 26 confirmed B-type hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and find that while most are consistent with ejection from the Milky Way's center via the Hills mechanism, HVS3 is highly likely to have originated in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). Its high relative velocity of 870 km/s during a 21.1 Myr close approach is only explainable by the Hills mechanism acting near a massive black hole in the LMC, requiring a central black hole of at least 4×10³–10⁴ M⊙—offering direct evidence for a massive black hole in the LMC.

source_fields.title: A hypervelocity star with a Magellanic origin

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: HVS3: HVS3 is consistent with being ejected from the center of the LMC at a velocity of 870 km/s and a time of 21.1 Myr ago, with its orbit remaining robust under uncertainties in the Milky Way potential and the Sun's motion. Conclusion: This study uses Gaia DR2 proper motions to confirm that HVS3 originated in the LMC, providing strong evidence for a massive black hole there through a Hills mechanism ejection event. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of re-evaluating the origin of HVS3 by demonstrating that, despite initial claims of an extragalactic origin, a Galactic center Hills mechanism with a massive black hole remains the most plausible and consistent explanation, resolving the longevity and kinematic paradox while reinforcing the validity of the Hills ejection model for confirmed B-type hypervelocity stars. Appendix A Properties of HVS: This section presents a comprehensive table of the physical and kinematic properties of 26 hypervelocity stars, including their proper motions, heliocentric velocities, distances, and references, with most data derived from Gaia DR2. Testing with mock simulations: This section uses mock N-body simulations of hypervelocity stars ejected from the LMC and Milky Way to test the accuracy of origin identification and find that most mock stars are inconsistent with an LMC origin, suggesting that observed HVSs likely do not originate from the LMC. Population of hypervelocity stars: This section describes the method and general properties of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by rewinding their orbits backward in the gravitational potentials of the Milky Way and the Large Magellanic Cloud, using observational data and Monte Carlo sampling to determine their closest approach points and origins.

abstract: Using proper motion measurements from Gaia DR2, we probe the origin of 26 previously known hypervelocity stars (HVSs) around the Milky Way.
We find that a significant fraction of these stars have a high probability of originating close to the Milky Way centre, but there is one obvious outlier.
title: A hypervelocity star with a Magellanic origin

catalog_assessment.evidence: Probes origin of 26 previously known hypervelocity stars using Gaia DR2 proper motions, focusing on HVS3 with detailed kinematic analysis and orbital history.

papers.title: Massive runaways and walkaway stars
papers.author_names: M. Renzo E. Zapartas S. D. Mink Y. Götberg S. Justham R. Farmer R. Izzard S. Toonen H. Sana

abstract.text: Anticipating the kinematic constraints from the Gaia mission, we perform an extensive numerical study of the evolution of massive binary systems to predict the peculiar velocities that stars obtain when their companion collapses and disrupts the system. Our aim is to (1) identify which predictions are robust against model uncertainties and assess their implications, (2) investigate which physical processes leave a clear imprint and may therefore be constrained observationally and (3) provide a suite of publicly available model predictions. We find that $22_{-8}^{+26}$% of all massive binary systems merge prior to the first core collapse in the system. Of the remainder, $86_{-9}^{+11}$% become unbound because of the core-collapse. Remarkably, this rarely produce runaway stars (i.e., stars with velocities above 30 km/s). These are outnumbered by more than an order of magnitude by slower unbound companions, or "walkaway stars". This is a robust outcome of our simulations and is due to the reversal of the mass ratio prior to the explosion and widening of the orbit, as we show analytically and numerically. We estimate a $10^{+5}_{-8}$% of massive stars to be walkaways and only $0.5^{+1.0}_{-0.4}$% to be runaways, nearly all of which have accreted mass from their companion. Our findings are consistent with earlier studies, however the low runaway fraction we find is in tension with observed fractions 10%. If Gaia confirms these high fractions of massive runaway stars resulting from binaries, it would imply that we are currently missing physics in the binary models. Finally, we show that high end of the mass distributions of runaway stars is very sensitive to the assumed black hole natal kicks and propose this as a potentially stringent test for the explosion mechanism. We discuss companions remaining bound which can evolve into X-ray and gravitational wave sources.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Numerical simulations of massive binary evolution reveal that most systems merge or become unbound before core collapse, with only a small fraction producing runaway stars (≤1%)—a result robust across model uncertainties. The majority of unbound companions are 'walkaway stars' with velocities below 30 km/s due to orbital widening and mass ratio reversal during core collapse, challenging observed runaway fractions (~10%). The high-mass tail of runaway stars is sensitive to black hole natal kicks, offering a testable prediction for explosion mechanisms, while bound companions may evolve into X-ray or gravitational wave sources.

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: The footnotes clarify methodological assumptions, limitations, and corrections in the study's stellar evolution and binary interaction modeling. Appendix#2: The population synthesis results include detailed binary evolution data for each parameter variation, with all output quantities weighted by system probability to compute accurate statistical averages and distributions. Appendix#3: Fig. C.1 and C.2 present the velocity distribution of dynamically ejected main-sequence stars, accounting for their remaining main-sequence lifetime and showing a minor velocity peak at high speeds linked to common envelope evolution without mass accretion. Appendix#4: This appendix presents pre-CC separation and mass distributions for binaries with a main-sequence companion, showing how mass transfer and interaction histories influence the final ejection velocities of supernova remnants. Conclusion: Our simulations show that binary disruption by supernova explosions primarily produces slow "walkaway" stars rather than runaway stars, with only a small fraction of massive stars exceeding 60 km/s, suggesting that observed O-type runaway fractions may be overestimated or require additional physical processes beyond current binary evolution models. Introduction: This paper introduces the concept of "walkaway stars" — slow, unbound main sequence companions left behind after binary disruption — as a novel observational signature of stellar-mass black hole formation, addressing the gap in detecting such black holes through their former companions' anomalous, low-velocity motions rather than rare accretion or merger events. Analytic estimates: Analytic estimates show that the ejection velocity of runaway and walkaway stars is approximately equal to their final pre-core-collapse orbital velocity, which can be derived from initial binary parameters using mass transfer and angular momentum conservation laws. Ejected companions: This section shows that in binary systems with a main-sequence companion, the vast majority of companions are ejected at velocities below 30 km/s (walkaway stars), with only a small fraction becoming runaways, and that the ejection velocities of massive companions remain typically below 60 km/s. Astrophysical Implications: Astrophysical velocity effects from parent association dispersion and systemic lag motions slightly alter the ejected star velocity distribution but do not significantly change the runaway fraction or reconcile observed values with simulations. Impact of model variations: The paper proposes a systematic sensitivity analysis method that varies one free parameter at a time while holding others constant to assess the robustness of binary disruption predictions, finding that most outcomes are insensitive to physical uncertainties except for specific cases like natal kicks, which can significantly alter disruption fractions. Binary population synthesis calculations: This section describes binary population synthesis calculations using the binary_c code to model isolated binary systems with varying initial mass, mass ratio, and orbital period distributions, assessing the sensitivity of results to key astrophysical assumptions while acknowledging limitations in accounting for parameter correlations. Systemic velocities of NS and BH binaries: The systemic velocities of NS and BH binaries remaining bound after core collapse are largely insensitive to model variations, with median velocities of ~20 km/s for NS+MS binaries and a bimodal distribution peaking at 0 km/s for BH+MS binaries, primarily due to fallback and natal kick effects. Example of the evolution of a binary system: This section describes the evolution of a binary system leading to the ejection of a companion after the first core-collapse supernova, highlighting mass transfer, orbital changes, and the role of natal kicks in determining the final fate of the system. A study of the kinematical imprints of the physical processes governing the evolution and explosion of their binary progenitors: This study investigates how the motion and dynamics of binary star systems influence the evolution and explosive outcomes of their progenitors.

abstract: Anticipating the kinematic constraints from the Gaia mission, we perform an extensive numerical study of the evolution of massive binary systems to predict the peculiar velocities that stars obtain when
Remarkably, this rarely produce runaway stars (i.e., stars with velocities above 30 km/s). These are outnumbered by more than an order of magnitude by slower unbound companions, or "walkaway stars".
We estimate a $10^{+5}_{-8}$% of massive stars to be walkaways and only $0.5^{+1.0}_{-0.4}$% to be runaways, nearly all of which have accreted mass from their companion.
If Gaia confirms these high fractions of massive runaway stars resulting from binaries, it would imply that we are currently missing physics in the binary models.

Finally, we show that high end of the mass distributions of runaway stars is very sensitive to the assumed black hole natal kicks and propose this as a potentially stringent test for the explosion mechanism
title: Massive runaways and walkaway stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/numerical study of massive binary evolution, predicting runaway/walkaway fractions. No observational stellar data or catalog presented.

papers.title: HST astrometry in the 30 Doradus region: II. Runaway stars from new proper motions in the Large Magellanic Cloud
papers.author_names: Imants Platais Daniel J. Lennon Roeland P. van der Marel Andrea Bellini Elena Sabbi Laura L. Watkins Sangmo T. Sohn Nolan R. Walborn Luigi R. Bedin Christopher J. Evans Selma E. de Mink Hugues Sana Artemio Herrero Norbert Langer Paul Crowther

abstract.text: We present a catalog of relative proper motions for 368,787 stars in the 30 Doradus region of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), based on a dedicated two-epoch survey with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) and supplemented with proper motions from our pilot archival study. We demonstrate that a relatively short epoch difference of 3 years is sufficient to reach a $\\\\sim$0.1 mas yr$^{-1}$ level of precision or better. A number of stars have relative proper motions exceeding a 3-sigma error threshold, representing a mixture of Milky Way denizens and 17 potential LMC runaway stars. Based upon 183 VFTS OB-stars with the best proper motions, we conclude that none of them move faster than $\\\\sim$0.3 mas yr$^{-1}$ in each coordinate -- equivalent to $\\\\sim$70 km s$^{-1}$. Among the remaining 351 VFTS stars with less accurate proper motions, only one candidate OB runaway can be identified. We rule out any OB star in our sample moving at a tangential velocity exceeding $\\\\sim$120 km s$^{-1}$. The most significant result of this study is finding 10 stars over wide range of masses, which appear to be ejected from the massive star cluster R136 in the tangential plane to angular distances from $35^{\\\\prime\\\\prime}$ out to $407^{\\\\prime\\\\prime}$, equivalent to 8-98 pc. The tangential velocities of these runaways appear to be correlated with apparent magnitude, indicating a possible dependence on the stellar mass.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A high-precision astrometric survey of 368,787 stars in the 30 Doradus region of the LMC using HST two-epoch observations reveals 10 potential runaway OB stars ejected from the R 136 cluster, with tangential velocities correlated to apparent magnitude—suggesting a mass-dependent ejection mechanism. The study achieves sub-0.1 mas yr⁻¹ proper motion precision via advanced PSF modeling and CTE correction, and rules out any OB star with tangential velocity exceeding ∼120 km s⁻¹, setting a stringent upper limit on runaway kinematics.

source_fields.title: HST astrometry in the 30 Doradus region: II. Runaway stars from new proper motions in the Large Magellanic Cloud

source_fields.sections_text: CONCLUSIONS: This study presents a high-accuracy proper motion catalog of 368,787 stars in 30 Doradus, identifying runaway stars likely ejected from the R 136 cluster and providing new insights into the dynamical ejection processes of massive stars in young stellar clusters. INTRODUCTION: This study presents a significantly improved catalog of high-precision relative proper motions for individual stars in the 30 Doradus region using a carefully matched two-epoch HST survey, addressing the problem of identifying runaway OB stars and refining models of massive star formation by overcoming systematic errors and spatial coverage limitations in prior work. DISCUSSION AND APPLICATIONS: The experiment finds that among VFTS OB stars, only VFTS 285 shows consistent proper motions supporting its status as an OB runaway, while most fast-moving stars are spurious due to measurement errors or binary systems, with proper motions ranging from 0.1 to 0.4 mas yr⁻¹ (equivalent to 25–100 km s⁻¹) and accuracy limited by HST instrument constraints and small data sets. HST SURVEY OF 30 DOR AND DATA REDUCTIONS: This section describes the HST survey of 30 Doradus using WFC3/UVIS and ACS/WFC in parallel, detailing observation strategies, data reductions, and the detection of differential charge-transfer inefficiency effects that could impact astrometric accuracy.

abstract: We present a catalog of relative proper motions for 368,787 stars in the 30 Doradus region of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), based on a dedicated two-epoch survey with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST
A number of stars have relative proper motions exceeding a 3-sigma error threshold, representing a mixture of Milky Way denizens and 17 potential LMC runaway stars.
Based upon 183 VFTS OB-stars with the best proper motions, we conclude that none of them move faster than $\\\\sim$0.3 mas yr$^{-1}$ in each coordinate -- equivalent to $\\\\sim$70 km s$^{-1}$.
Among the remaining 351 VFTS stars with less accurate proper motions, only one candidate OB runaway can be identified.

The most significant result of this study is finding 10 stars over wide range of masses, which appear to be ejected from the massive star cluster R136 in the tangential plane to angular distances from
title: Runaway stars from new proper motions in the Large Magellanic Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Presents catalog of relative proper motions for 368,787 stars from HST observations, identifies 17 potential LMC runaway stars and 10 stars ejected from R136 cluster.

papers.title: Constraining Solar position and velocity with a Nearby Hypervelocity Star
papers.author_names: Kohei Hattori Monica Valluri Norberto Castro (University of Michigan)

abstract.text: Gravitational 3-body interaction among binary stars and the supermassive black hole (SMBH) at the center of the Milky Way occasionally ejects a hypervelocity star (HVS) with a velocity of ~1000 km/s. Due to the ejection location, such a HVS initially has negligible azimuthal angular momentum Lz ~ 0 kpc km/s. Even if the halo is mildly triaxial, Lz of a recently ejected nearby HVS remains negligible, since its flight time from the Galactic Center is too short to accumulate noticeable torque. However, if we make a wrong assumption about the Solar position and velocity, such a HVS would apparently have noticeable non-zero azimuthal angular momentum, due to the wrong reflex motion of the Sun. Conversely, with precise astrometric data for a nearby HVS, we can measure the Solar position and velocity by assuming that the HVS has zero azimuthal angular momentum. Based on this idea, here we propose a method to estimate the Galactocentric distance of the Sun R0 and the Galactocentric Solar azimuthal velocity Vsun by using a HVS. We demonstrate with mock data for a nearby HVS candidate that the Gaia astrometric data, along with the currently available constraint on Vsun/R0 from the proper motion measurement of Sgr A*, can constrain R0 and Vsun with uncertainties of ~0.27 kpc and ~7.8 km/s (or fractional uncertainties of 3 percent), respectively. Our method will be a promising tool to constrain (R0, Vsun), given that Gaia is expected to discover many nearby HVSs in the near future.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This paper proposes a novel dynamical method to constrain the Sun's Galactocentric distance $R_0$ and azimuthal velocity $V_\odot$ using the azimuthal angular momentum of nearby hypervelocity stars (HVSs). By assuming that HVSs ejected from the Galactic Center have negligible $L_z$ (due to short flight times), any apparent non-zero $L_z$ in observations is attributed to a mismodeling of the Sun's motion; thus, precise astrometric data from Gaia can be used to invert for $R_0$ and $V_\odot$ independently. The method achieves fractional uncertainties of ~3% in $R_0$ and ~7.8 km/s in $V_\odot$, leveraging the known $V_\odot/R_0$ ratio from Sgr A* proper motion measurements.

source_fields.title: Constraining Solar position and velocity with a Nearby Hypervelocity Star

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: This paper presents a Bayesian method to precisely constrain the solar position $R_0$ and velocity $V_\\odot$ using a single nearby hypervelocity star ejected from the Galactic Center, with mock analyses showing significant improvements over existing methods when combined with Gaia data and Sgr A* proper motion. Discussion: The method's assumption of negligible azimuthal angular momentum ($L_z \\simeq 0$) for hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from the Galactic Center is valid, as mock simulations show that even in a triaxial halo, $|L_z|$ remains typically less than 2% of the Sun’s value, supporting reliable estimates of $(R_0, V_\\odot)$ with minimal bias. Formulation: This section presents a Bayesian framework to estimate the Galactic center distance $R_0$ and solar vertical velocity $V_\\odot$ by leveraging the astrometric and kinematic data of a nearby hypervelocity star, exploiting the constraint that its azimuthal angular momentum is nearly zero due to its origin at the Galactic Center. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel dynamical method using hypervelocity stars ejected from the Galactic Center to provide an independent constraint on the Sun's position and velocity, complementing the existing $V_{\\odot}/R_{0}$ measurement from Sgr A* proper motion and thereby enabling more precise individual determination of $R_0$ and $V_{\\odot}$. Analyses of mock data: The Bayesian analysis of Gaia-like mock data demonstrates that the method accurately recovers the correct solar position and velocity within tight uncertainties, with performance primarily limited by distance measurement errors and improved by better distance or proper motion precision. Mock Gaiadata of LAMOST-HVS1: This section presents a mock Gaia dataset for the nearby hypervelocity star LAMOST-HVS1, assuming it was ejected from the Galactic Center, to test and demonstrate the effectiveness of the method for estimating the solar position and velocity using high-precision astrometric data. Appendix A Non-Gaussian error distribution for the distance modulus: This section argues that assuming a Gaussian error distribution for the distance modulus is unrealistic and presents a more general likelihood expression that accounts for non-Gaussian errors in the distance modulus. Appendix B Performance of our method as a function of observational uncertainties: The paper proposes a Bayesian analysis method that evaluates how improvements in distance modulus and proper motion measurement uncertainties affect the inferred solar position and velocity $(R_0, V_\\odot)$ by analyzing mock data from a nearby hypervelocity star, showing that better distance or proper motion measurements significantly improve the precision of $R_0$ up to a point, with proper motion errors having a stronger impact when distance uncertainties are moderate.

abstract: Gravitational 3-body interaction among binary stars and the supermassive black hole (SMBH) at the center of the Milky Way occasionally ejects a hypervelocity star (HVS) with a velocity of ~1000 km/s.
title: Constraining Solar position and velocity with a Nearby Hypervelocity Star

catalog_assessment.evidence: Method paper proposing use of HVSs to constrain Solar parameters. Demonstrates with mock data, no actual observational catalog presented.

papers.title: A search for Galactic runaway stars using Gaia Data Release 1 and Hipparcos proper motions
papers.author_names: J. Maíz Apellániz M. Pantaleoni González R. H. Barbá S. Simón-Díaz I. Negueruela D. J. Lennon A. Sota E. Trigueros Páez

abstract.text: CONTEXT.The first Gaia Data Release (DR1) significantly improved the previously available proper motions for the majority of the Tycho-2 stars. AIMS. We want to detect runaway stars using Gaia DR1 proper motions and compare our results with previous searches. METHODS. Runaway O stars and BA supergiants are detected using a 2-D proper-motion method. The sample is selected using Simbad, spectra from our GOSSS project, literature spectral types, and photometry processed using CHORIZOS. RESULTS. We detect 76 runaway stars, 17 (possibly 19) of them with no prior identification as such, with an estimated detection rate of approximately one half of the real runaway fraction. An age effect appears to be present, with objects of spectral subtype B1 and later having travelled for longer distances than runaways of earlier subtypes. We also tentatively propose that the fraction of runaways is lower among BA supergiants that among O stars but further studies using future Gaia data releases are needed to confirm this. The frequency of fast rotators is high among runaway O stars, which indicates that a significant fraction of them (and possibly a majority) is produced in supernova explosions.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A 2D proper-motion analysis of Galactic massive stars using Gaia DR1 and Hipparcos data identifies 76 runaway stars, 17 of which are newly detected, with a detection rate of ~50% of the true runaway fraction. Runaways of spectral subtype B1 and later show longer travel distances, suggesting age-dependent ejection mechanisms; BA supergiants have a lower runaway fraction than O stars, though this requires further validation. A high frequency of fast rotators among runaway O stars supports supernova-driven ejection as a dominant formation mechanism.

source_fields.title: A search for Galactic runaway stars using Gaia Data Release 1 and Hipparcos proper motions

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study identifies 76 runaway star candidates, assigns spectral and temperature classifications, and finds evidence of age-related differences and faster rotation among runaway O stars, suggesting a possible dominance of supernova explosion scenarios over multi-body interactions. Analysis: The analysis shows that the 2-D method effectively identifies runaway O stars with large tangential velocities but misses those with dominant radial motion, while false positives are expected to be rare due to stringent spectral and astrometric criteria. Introduction: This paper innovates by extending the identification of runaway stars to include BA supergiants and refining the sample with new, high-quality spectroscopic data from GOSSS, thereby solving the problem of false positives in O-star identifications and enabling more reliable detection of true runaway stars. Data and methods: The paper proposes a method that uses Gaia DR1 and Hipparcos proper motions—after cleaning and cross-matching O-type and BA supergiant stars from Simbad—to identify Galactic runaway stars by analyzing their motion, with additional spectral and photometric validation to reduce false positives. Runaway candidates: This section presents a catalog of O-type runaway stars previously identified in Paper I, categorized by their proper motion deviations and sourced from either Gaia (TGAS) or Hipparcos data, with references to prior studies.

abstract: CONTEXT.The first Gaia Data Release (DR1) significantly improved the previously available proper motions for the majority of the Tycho-2 stars. AIMS.
We want to detect runaway stars using Gaia DR1 proper motions and compare our results with previous searches. METHODS.
Runaway O stars and BA supergiants are detected using a 2-D proper-motion method.
We detect 76 runaway stars, 17 (possibly 19) of them with no prior identification as such, with an estimated detection rate of approximately one half of the real runaway fraction.
The frequency of fast rotators is high among runaway O stars, which indicates that a significant fraction of them (and possibly a majority) is produced in supernova explosions.
title: A search for Galactic runaway stars using Gaia Data Release 1 and Hipparcos proper motions

catalog_assessment.evidence: Detects 76 runaway stars using Gaia DR1 proper motions, 17 newly identified, with sample selection using SIMBAD, spectral types, and photometry.

papers.title: On the kinematics of a runaway Be star population

abstract.text: We explore the hypothesis that B type emission-line stars (Be stars) have their origin in mass-transfer binaries by measuring the fraction of runaway Be stars. We assemble the largest-to-date catalogue of 632 Be stars with 6D kinematics, exploiting the precise astrometry of the Tycho-Gaia Astrometric Solution (TGAS) from the first Gaia Data Release. Using binary stellar evolution simulations, we make predictions for the runaway and equatorial rotation velocities of a runaway Be star population. Accounting for observational biases, we calculate that if all classical Be stars originated through mass transfer in binaries, then $17.5\\\\%$ of the Be stars in our catalogue should be runaways. The remaining $82.5\\\\%$ should be in binaries with subdwarfs, white dwarfs or neutron stars, because those systems either remained bound post-supernova or avoided the supernova entirely. Using a Bayesian methodology, we compare the hypothesis that each Be star in our catalogue is a runaway to the null hypothesis that it is a member of the Milky Way disc. We find that $13.1^{+2.6}_{-2.4}\\\\%$ of the Be stars in our catalogue are runaways, and identify a subset of 40 high-probability runaways. We argue that deficiencies in our understanding of binary stellar evolution, as well as the degeneracy between velocity dispersion and number of runaway stars, can explain the slightly lower runaway fraction. We thus conclude that all Be stars could be explained by an origin in mass-transfer binaries. This conclusion is testable with the second Gaia data release (DR2).

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section presents and combines multiple datasets of Be stars with precise radial velocities, proper motions, and distances to study their origins, highlighting improved kinematic accuracy from TGAS and a more refined understanding of runaway star populations. Discussion: The method successfully identifies 17.5% of Be stars as likely runaway stars, with high posterior probabilities (ranging from 90.8% to 99.8%) and median runaway velocities between 20–80 km/s, and the expected false positive rate of ~4 stars is consistent with the analysis. Conclusions: This work demonstrates that approximately 13–17% of Be stars are runaways, consistent with their origin through binary mass transfer, using a large kinematic catalogue and Bayesian modeling of Gaia data. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of providing strong observational evidence for the post-mass-transfer model of Be star formation by identifying runaway Be stars in supernova remnants and linking their properties to binary evolution and mass transfer, thereby solving the long-standing problem of how rapidly rotating Be stars acquire their high angular momentum and form decretion discs. Bayesian Framework: This section presents a Bayesian mixture model to estimate the fraction of Be stars that are runaways or outliers by accounting for disk kinematics, runaway velocities, and observational uncertainties, concluding that only a small fraction of Be stars exceed 40 km s⁻¹ in peculiar velocity, supporting the post-mass-transfer origin for most Be stars. The Fraction Of Be Runaways: This section outlines the Bayesian modeling approach used to infer Be star runaway velocities, emphasizing the challenges of parameter degeneracies and the importance of data precision in breaking the degeneracy between velocity dispersions and the runaway fraction. The Post-Mass-Transfer Channel For Be Stars: This section uses binary stellar population synthesis to model Be stars formed via the post-mass-transfer channel, linking their observed velocity and spectral properties to their binary evolutionary history.

abstract: We explore the hypothesis that B type emission-line stars (Be stars) have their origin in mass-transfer binaries by measuring the fraction of runaway Be stars.
Accounting for observational biases, we calculate that if all classical Be stars originated through mass transfer in binaries, then $17.5\\\\%$ of the Be stars in our catalogue should be runaways.
We find that $13.1^{+2.6}_{-2.4}\\\\%$ of the Be stars in our catalogue are runaways, and identify a subset of 40 high-probability runaways.

We argue that deficiencies in our understanding of binary stellar evolution, as well as the degeneracy between velocity dispersion and number of runaway stars, can explain the slightly lower runaway fraction
We thus conclude that all Be stars could be explained by an origin in mass-transfer binaries. This conclusion is testable with the second Gaia data release (DR2).

catalog_assessment.evidence: Assembles catalogue of 632 Be stars with 6D kinematics from TGAS/Gaia DR1, identifies 40 high-probability runaways, and measures runaway fraction.
